ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'on whom you have set your hope' presents a factual situation (you have actually set your hope on Moses) without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in 'Do not think' applies to the matrix verb 'think', not to the target gram 'have set your hope', which appears in a relative clause describing Moses and is itself positive.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'on whom you have set your hope' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have set your hope' denotes a bounded activity (the act of placing hope) where the focus is on the occurrence of this experience in the subject's life history rather than on a persisting resulting state, matching the intuition and the third 'set_to_1_if' condition for perception/communication-type verbs focused on experiential occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set' describes a change of state (placing hope upon someone), and the clause indicates that this resulting state—having hope directed toward Moses—is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete mental act of setting hope on Moses rather than a continuous state holding throughout a time span, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'you have set your hope' presents the act of setting hope as a single bounded, completed occurrence (a transition from not having set hope to having set it), rather than as an ongoing state or habitual action.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is a present perfect asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (you have already set your hope on Moses), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context that would mark it as irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have set your hope' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by the past participle 'set', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have set your hope' is active voice with 'have' as a perfect auxiliary and 'set' as a transitive past participle; the grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent (doer), not the patient, and there is no 'been' auxiliary or passive morphology present.

FullText: They gathered twelve baskets of leftovers after everyone had eaten .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' lacks an overt internal argument (direct object); the object is implied but not syntactically realized, matching the activity reading pattern in the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'eat' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'everyone'), and although the direct object is not overt in this clause, the verb inherently introduces an external argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' licenses an internal argument (the understood direct object 'food/meal'), making it transitive and thus not unergative.
AktAcc_explanation: Eating is a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint (consuming a meal to completion), making it an accomplishment that can be measured with 'in X time' adverbials.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a durative process of consuming food over measurable time, not a punctual transition into a new state; eating unfolds through phases and cannot be naturally modified by a time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can (e.g., 'at noon' specifies when eating occurred, not when a boundary transition happened).
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint (completion of eating) and a defined result state (food consumed), satisfying the set_to_0_if condition for situations entailing specific endpoints.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had eaten' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having eaten to having eaten) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had eaten' denotes a bounded dynamic event with a clear endpoint (completion of eating), involving internal eventive unfolding and change-of-state, which violates the stative requirement of AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'after everyone had eaten' presents a factual past event that actually occurred (they gathered leftovers because people had eaten), using past perfect for temporal anteriority within a narrative, not expressing a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had eaten' is not negated; it describes a realized event (everyone completed eating), and no negator applies to this verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the clausal past time anchor 'after', a temporal conjunction that explicitly establishes a specific past time interval for the event 'had eaten'.
DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had eaten' presents the eating event as part of the narrative history without asserting a resulting state; it answers whether the eating occurred (it did), and the focus is on the completion of the bounded activity itself, not on a persisting consequence.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'eat' describes an activity/consumption event that does not entail a resulting state persisting at the reference time; the clause merely reports a past completed action without indicating that a changed state currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had eaten' describes a discrete, bounded event (the completion of eating) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the transition into a new state (finishing eating) before the leftovers were gathered.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had eaten' is a transition event (completion of eating) construed as a bounded whole that occurred prior to the gathering of leftovers, functioning as a discrete step in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had eaten' asserts a realized fact in the actual world (the eating occurred before the gathering of leftovers); it is not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual, and no modal, conditional, or irrealis context applies.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, which matches the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for past perfect forms, overriding the 'set_to_0_if' condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had eaten' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'eaten' of an intransitive verb); the grammatical subject 'everyone' is the semantic agent (the doer), not the patient, and there is no 'been' auxiliary or by-phrase required for passive voice.

FullText: They boarded a boat to travel over the sea to Capernaum . It was dark and Jesus had not yet come to them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'come' has no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it is used intransitively with only an overt subject ('Jesus'), matching the set_to_0_if condition for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Jesus) undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criterion for motion verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an internal argument ('to them'), and denotes a change-of-location event where the subject functions as the theme/patient moving toward a destination rather than as an external agent initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'come' here denotes a punctual boundary transition (arrival at a location), not a durative process leading toward a goal, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival/presence), naturally modified by the time-point adverbial 'yet' (indicating a defined temporal boundary), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint (arrival at a location), and the negation 'had not yet come' describes a single bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of awareness that Jesus had not arrived, lacking dynamic durative unfolding.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'come' entails a transition into a new resulting state (Jesus arriving/being present), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing resulting change of state should be marked 0.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had not yet come' is marked with past perfect progressive aspect and denotes a bounded eventive change (the arrival of Jesus), which involves dynamic progression and an inherent endpoint, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and event boundaries.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had not yet come to them' presents a factual past situation (using past perfect for temporal anteriority in narrative) without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the situation is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not yet come', which directly negates the eventuality of Jesus coming, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the lexical past time adverb 'yet', which explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval by indicating that the action had not occurred up to that point in the narrative.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had not yet come' functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's history up to that point, which is a core instantiation of DtExp according to the set_to_1_if criteria.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' is negated ('had not yet come'), so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the clause negates the occurrence of the event.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had not yet come' describes a discrete bounded event (Jesus's arrival) that has not occurred by the reference time, focusing on the transition/non-occurrence of an event rather than a continuous situation holding over a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not yet', indicating the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which vetoes perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not yet come' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Jesus had not arrived at that point in the narrative), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet come' is an active past perfect construction with 'come' as an intransitive verb; it lacks the passive auxiliary sequence 'have/had' + 'been' + past participle, and the subject 'Jesus' is the semantic agent (the one performing the action of coming), not the semantic patient.

FullText: » Do not work for food that perishes but for food that offers everlasting life . The Son of man will give you this food . The Father , even God , has set his seal upon him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has set' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The Father') and an overt direct object ('his seal'), with a prepositional phrase ('upon him') indicating the affected internal argument, satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive construction.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an external argument (the Father/God as agent) and introduces a direct object ('his seal'), satisfying set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'set' licenses an internal argument ('his seal') as a direct object, triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The act of setting a seal upon someone is a punctual boundary transition (an instantaneous act of authorization or approval) without a durative process phase leading to completion, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'has set his seal upon' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the Father's approval/authorization of the Son), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('at that moment'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot meaningfully stop setting a seal'), and lacks measurable internal duration, all satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'has set his seal upon him' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (the seal being set), which entails an inherent endpoint and is not a durative, ongoing activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has set his seal upon him' entails a resulting change of state (the seal is now set, marking a transition from unsealed to sealed condition), which violates the set_to_1_if condition and satisfies set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'has set his seal upon' denotes a bounded perfective event involving a change-of-state (the seal being placed/established), which is dynamic and eventive rather than a stative condition holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The Father, even God, has set his seal upon him' presents a situation as actually occurring in the actual world (a factual statement about God's action), with no indication that it is contrary to fact, counterfactual, or expressing an unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has set his seal upon' is not negated; the negation 'Do not work' in the sentence applies to a different clause and does not negate the target gram.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'has set his seal upon him' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'set his seal upon' entails a change of state (the Father marking/authorizing the Son) where that resulting state of authorization/approval holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for resulting states.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'set his seal upon' describes a change of status (divine authorization/approval), and the context indicates this resulting state (the seal being set) currently holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has set his seal upon' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the action of sealing) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'set his seal upon' is a transition event (a completed action of marking/authorizing) construed as a bounded whole, and the present perfect form presents this as a single completed occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is in present perfect tense asserting a realized action in the actual world, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has set his seal upon' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'set' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'The Father' is the semantic agent (doer) performing the action of setting a seal, and 'him' is the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no 'have been + past participle' auxiliary sequence.

FullText: Jesus answered : » This is the work of God , that you have an active faith in the one whom he has sent . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject (he) and an overt direct object (the one/whom), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an external argument (the agent 'he' who performs the sending action), which violates the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has sent' licenses an internal argument (the implied object 'him'), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the act of dispatching/commissioning someone) without a durative process phase leading to a defined result state, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending is complete), can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial ('he sent at that moment'), does not permit interruption ('one cannot meaningfully stop sending'), and lacks measurable internal duration, representing a boundary transition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a completed action with a specific result state (the one whom he has sent = a definite person now in a state of having been sent), which entails an inherent endpoint and thus fails the activity criteria.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has sent' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-sent to sent), which violates the set_to_0_if condition and disqualifies it from being semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a bounded dynamic event (sending is a change-of-state action with an inherent endpoint), which triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and eventive verbs, incompatible with AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'the one whom he has sent' presents a factual situation (God has sent someone) as actually occurring in the world described by the text, with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not occur.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has sent' is not negated; no negator ('not', 'n't', 'never', or 'neither...nor') directly modifies it, and the clause presents the sending as a realized event within a positive relative clause.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'the one whom he has sent' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has sent' denotes a change of state (God sending the one) where the resulting state—that God has an agent/messenger in the world—is relevant to the current discourse context and theological claim being made.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a past action of sending without entailing a resulting state that persists at the reference time; it reports a completed past event (God sent someone) rather than a transition to a new state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'has sent' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of sending) resulting in a new state, focusing on the transition rather than a continuous situation holding over time, which violates the set_to_0_if condition.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has sent' is a transition event (send → sent) construed as a completed whole, presenting God's action of sending as a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has sent' asserts a realized fact within the actual world (God's action of sending), embedded in a relative clause that identifies 'the one' as a realized entity, with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical marking.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has sent' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'sent', with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has sent' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'he' as the semantic agent (doer) and the semantic patient 'one' as the grammatical object; it lacks the passive morphology 'have + been + past participle' required for VcPass.

FullText: » I told you . You have seen me and yet do not believe .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('me'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with two overt participants.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an external argument (the subject 'You') and introduces a direct object ('me'), violating the unaccusative requirement that no external argument be licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have seen' licenses an internal argument ('me' as direct object), triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Have seen' is a perceptual/cognitive verb construed here as a punctual transition into visual awareness rather than a durative process leading to a defined result state, and it can be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a bounded goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (seeing/perceiving me), which is a crossing of a definable threshold from not-seeing to seeing, satisfying the criterion for punctual perceptual cognitive transition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing/perceiving the speaker), which is treated as a single completed occurrence rather than a durative activity, matching the negative example 'They have heard the blasphemy.'
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have seen' entails a resulting state of perceptual knowledge and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence; it describes an experience with lasting relevance rather than a bounded instantaneous event.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the completion of awareness (seeing), which encodes eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' presents a situation as having actually occurred in the actual world (factual past experience), with no counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that the event did not happen.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have seen' is not negated; the negation 'do not believe' applies to a different verb and does not negate the eventuality of seeing.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception verb focusing on the occurrence of an experience (having witnessed the subject) at least once in the subject's life history, without asserting a resulting state, matching the intuition and set_to_1_if criteria for experiential perfect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'see' is a perception event that does not entail a resulting state holding at the reference time; it reports a past perceptual experience without a persisting change of state, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have seen me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of seeing) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, with no duration adverbials or habitual markers indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have seen' denotes a stative situation (perception/knowledge state) without a construed boundary or transition, and the clause does not introduce a transition into or out of that state, matching the set_to_0_if criterion for stative situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world ('You have seen me'), with no modal, conditional, future, or wish context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have seen' lacks the required 'been' auxiliary and past participle structure for passive voice; it is active voice with the grammatical subject 'You' as the semantic agent performing the action of seeing.

FullText: » This is the will of him that sent me that I should not lose any of those he has given me , but I should raise them up at the last day .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has given' introduces an overt grammatical subject (implied 'he') and an overt direct object ('any of those'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event with external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an external argument (the implied agent 'he') and introduces a direct object ('any of those'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition for verbs that license external arguments and contain overt objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an internal argument ('any of those he has given me'), triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that vetoes ASUnrg annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has given' here denotes a punctual act of transferring or entrusting people to someone, without a durative process phase leading to a defined culmination, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/assignment) that occurred at a definable temporal boundary and entails a result state (the people are now given/assigned to the speaker), satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer with a specific result state (those people now belong to the speaker), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition of entailing a defined result state, and lacks the dynamic, durative, homogeneous unfolding required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-possessed to possessed), which violates the set_to_0_if condition and disqualifies it from being a semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a bounded dynamic event (transfer of possession) marking a completed action with an internal endpoint, which is incompatible with stative readings and triggers the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and dynamic activity.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' presents a situation as having actually occurred (those given to the speaker exist as a factual matter), not as contrary to fact, and occurs within a statement of divine will rather than a counterfactual conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'not' in the clause negates 'lose', not the target gram 'has given'; the target gram describes a completed action (those he has given me) that is presented as realized, not negated.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that explicitly anchors the event to a specific past time interval; the temporal reference is implicit in the perfect aspect, not established by an adverbial.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has given' entails a change of possession (transfer of people to the subject) that remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent clause 'I should raise them up,' which presupposes the subject currently possesses those given to him.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has given' describes a past transfer of possession without entailing a persisting resulting state at the reference time; it reports a past interaction (God giving people to the speaker) rather than a state currently in effect, matching the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'he has given me' describes a discrete past event (the act of giving) with focus on the transition into a state of possession, not a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has given' construes the act of giving as a completed, bounded event with a construed endpoint, presenting a discrete transition (the transfer of persons to the speaker) as a whole rather than as ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' asserts a realized state of affairs (those whom the sender has already given to the speaker) within the actual world, despite appearing in a clause expressing divine will and obligation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has given' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by the past participle 'given' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has given' is active voice (subject 'he' is the semantic agent/giver), not passive; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and the grammatical object 'me' is the semantic patient, not the subject.

FullText: » It is written in the prophets : Jehovah shall teach them all . Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has learned' appears without an overt internal argument (direct object); the clause reads 'Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me', where no affected patient is syntactically realised, matching the activity reading pattern in set_to_0_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has learned' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'one' who performs the learning action), making it agentive rather than unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has learned' licenses an internal argument (the object of learning, implied from context as 'from the Father'), satisfying the 'set_to_0_if' condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: "Has learned" denotes a durative process of acquiring knowledge directed toward a defined result state (having learned/mastered something), analogous to 'write a letter' or 'build a house', and can be modified by 'in X time', supporting an accomplishment reading.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a punctual transition into a new cognitive state (acquiring knowledge/understanding), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials ('has learned by noon'), and represents a boundary crossing into awareness rather than an unfolding durative process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a bounded cognitive event with an inherent endpoint (acquisition of knowledge), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for 'bounded perceptual or cognitive event marking the onset of awareness.'
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has learned' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence, thus failing the set_to_1_if condition and satisfying set_to_0_if.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'has learned' is morphologically marked as a perfective construction indicating a completed change-of-state (acquisition of knowledge), which involves dynamic progression and an event boundary, thus failing the stative criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me' presents a factual situation as actually occurring (generic/habitual present), not a counterfactual condition contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has learned' is not negated; it appears in a positive conditional clause ('Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me') where the negation feature does not apply.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has learned' denotes a bounded cognitive/perceptual activity where the focus is on having undergone the experience of learning from the Father at least once, without asserting a resulting state that persists at reference time; it answers whether the event has occurred in the subject's history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has learned' describes a change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing) where the resulting state of possessing knowledge currently holds at the reference time, as evidenced by the parallel structure 'hears the Father and has learned comes to me' indicating the learned state is in effect when the person comes.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has learned' describes a discrete bounded event (the completion of learning) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the transition into the state of having learned, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has learned' presents learning as a completed, bounded event that serves as a discrete precondition in the narrative sequence ('Every one who hears the Father and has learned comes to me'), construing the learning as a single accomplished whole rather than an ongoing or habitual state.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has learned' asserts a realized state in the actual world within a relative clause describing those who come to the speaker; it is not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has learned' is formed with present-tense 'has' followed by past participle 'learned' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form specified in set_to_0_if.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has learned' is active voice (subject 'one' is the semantic agent/learner), not passive; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure and does not express the subject as a semantic patient.

FullText: » No one has seen the Father . Only he who is from God , He has seen the Father .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'has seen' introduces an overt grammatical subject '(no one)' and an overt direct object 'the Father', satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'no one') and introduces a direct object ('the Father'), triggering the set_to_0_if conditions for both external argument licensing and overt object presence.

VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of transitive verb 'do'), with the grammatical subject 'he' functioning as the semantic agent, not the patient; no passive morphology ('been') is present.

FullText: He spoke these words at the treasury as he taught in the temple . No one tried to arrest him because his hour had not yet arrived .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'arrived' is intransitive and does not introduce an overt internal argument (direct or indirect object), matching the set_to_0_if condition for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument ('his hour') undergoes displacement with no external argument licensed, matching the set_to_1_if condition for unaccusative verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a change-of-location event where the sole argument 'his hour' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument initiating an activity, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-location verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Arrived' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment his hour comes) without a durative process phase, matching the achievement pattern explicitly listed as a negative example.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'arrive' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the moment when 'his hour' comes), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'has not yet arrived' denotes a specific, inherent endpoint (the arrival event itself), which is a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset of a defined state, not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'arrive' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from not-arrived to arrived), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that excludes events entailing change of state.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'has not yet arrived' denotes a bounded change-of-state event (the onset of arrival), which involves dynamic progression toward a completion point, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state and event boundaries.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'his hour had not yet arrived' expresses a factual past state within a narrative (temporal anteriority), not a counterfactual condition; the situation is presented as having actually occurred in the actual world.
ClNegPol_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is negated with 'not' in 'had not yet arrived', which directly negates the eventuality of arrival, satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains 'yet', which is a vague temporal adverbial that does not establish a specific past time interval; it only indicates incompleteness relative to an unspecified reference point, failing the requirement for an overt anchor to a specific past time.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'arrive' entails a change of location/state (the hour coming/arriving), and the resulting state (the hour not yet being present) is relevant to the discourse context explaining why no one arrested him.
DtRes_explanation: The negation 'had not yet arrived' negates the occurrence of the event itself, so no transition to a resulting state is entailed; the clause does not indicate that a change of state has occurred.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a transition into a new state (arrival as a punctual event) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the focus is on the non-occurrence of that transition, not on duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause is negated with 'not yet' (has not yet arrived), indicating that the transition has not occurred at the reference time and the situation remains open, which triggers the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has not yet arrived' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (his hour had not yet come at that past moment), with negation and temporal specification but no modal, conditional, future, or wish context that would mark it as irrealis.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not yet arrived' is an active intransitive verb (past perfect of 'arrive'), not a passive construction; it lacks the required auxiliary sequence 'have/has' + 'been' + past participle, and the subject 'hour' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not a patient.

FullText: » Who are you , « they asked . Jesus replied : » What have I been telling you from the beginning ?
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have been telling' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'I') and introduces a direct object ('you'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions for agentive verbs with objects.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'telling' licenses an internal argument ('you'), which is the recipient/indirect object, triggering the 'set_to_0_if' condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been telling' is dynamic and durative but lacks an inherent endpoint or result state, functioning as an ongoing communicative activity that can be modified by 'for X time' without implying completion of a defined goal.
AktAch_explanation: The present perfect progressive 'have been telling' denotes an ongoing durative process unfolding over measurable duration ('from the beginning'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and satisfies the set_to_0_if condition that the event unfolds over measurable duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' denotes a dynamic, durative activity of ongoing communication that unfolds over time without an inherent endpoint; it can be naturally modified by 'for X time' (e.g., 'telling you for years'), is homogeneous (any subinterval is still telling), and lacks a bounded result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have been telling' unfolds over a measurable duration (from the beginning to the present) rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, and it entails a repeated iterative series of telling actions, thus failing the semelfactive criterion.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (present perfect progressive), which entails dynamic unfolding and is explicitly incompatible with stative readings per the set_to_0_if rule.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation that has actually occurred (Jesus has been telling them things from the beginning), without any indication of counterfactuality, regret, or unfulfilled wishes.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been telling' is not negated; it appears in a direct question without negation markers ('not', 'n't', negative quantifiers, or 'never'), so ClNegPol does not apply.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'from the beginning' is a source/origin marker, not a temporal anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have been telling' denotes communication/perception where the focus is on the occurrence of repeated telling throughout a period ('from the beginning'), answering whether the event has occurred, without asserting a resulting state currently holds.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'telling' in 'have been telling you from the beginning' reports a past interaction without entailing that any directive or information is currently in effect; it merely describes what Jesus has communicated over time, not a persisting resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What have I been telling you from the beginning?' contains the duration adverbial 'from the beginning' indicating a situation (telling) that extends continuously from a past starting point to the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for duration adverbials.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' has the form 'have been' + present participle (-ing), which profiles the internal temporal development of the situation rather than presenting it as a bounded whole, triggering set_to_0_if condition 1.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' appears in a direct question asserting a realized fact about what Jesus has actually been communicating from the beginning, not projecting a hypothetical or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have been telling' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle ('telling'), which matches the set_to_1_if condition for progressive perfect forms, making it a tense-shifted perfect rather than a simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have been telling' has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + present participle (not past participle), and 'telling' is a non-passive progressive form; moreover, the grammatical subject 'I' is the semantic agent (the one doing the telling), not the patient, making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: Jesus said : » When you have lifted up impaled the Son of man you will know that I am that person . I do nothing by myself ! I speak what the Father taught me !

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have lifted up' licenses an external argument (the implied agent 'you' who performs the action of lifting/impaling), and the clause contains a direct object ('the Son of man'), both of which trigger set_to_0_if conditions.

DtRes_explanation: The clause is a temporal conditional ('When you have lifted up...you will know') that describes a future event as a precondition; the resulting state of impalement is not asserted to hold at the reference time, and the construction reports a past action without entailing a persisting state in the present.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (the lifting up/impalement of the Son of man) that triggers a consequent state ('you will know'), focusing on the transition and its result rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have lifted up' construes the transition event of impalement as a completed bounded whole, functioning as a discrete step in a conditional narrative sequence ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), which instantiates TLAspPfv.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' appears in a temporal conditional construction ('When you have lifted up...you will know'), projecting a future event as the condition for a consequent realization, which construes the situation as non-actual and hypothetical rather than asserted fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have lifted up' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'you' is the semantic agent performing the action of lifting up/impaling; the semantic patient 'the Son of man' is the grammatical object, making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: » He sent me and is with me . He has not left me alone . I always do the things that are pleasing to him ! «

ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He has not left me alone' presents a situation as actually occurring in the present/recent past without any counterfactual marking, conditional structure, or indication that it is contrary to fact.

FullText: They answered him : » We are Abraham’s seed , and have never been in bondage to any man . How can you say we will be made free ? «

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'been' (copula) licenses an internal argument ('in bondage'), and the sole argument functions as a theme/patient in a state-of-being construction rather than as an external agent/initiator.

AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' denotes a state or condition (being in bondage) rather than a dynamic, durative activity; it lacks the sustained dynamic engagement required for AktAct and represents a stative situation without inherent unfolding.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have never been in bondage' describes a state or condition (being in bondage) rather than a punctual dynamic occurrence; it entails a resulting state and unfolds over a measurable duration, failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have never been in bondage' encodes a bounded perceptual/cognitive event marking the onset or completion of a state (never experiencing bondage), which involves change-of-state semantics and eventive boundaries rather than a static stative condition.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval; 'never' is a negation/frequency marker, not a past time anchor.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have never been in bondage' explicitly contains the frequency adverb 'never' and functions as a claim about the absence of the event in the subject's life history, satisfying the set_to_1_if condition for negative Perfects and frequency adverbs.
DtRes_explanation: The clause uses a stative copula construction ('have been in bondage') with a negation and frequency adverbial ('never'), describing a persistent state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause contains the duration adverbial 'never' (negated universal quantifier) and denotes a non-terminating state of not being in bondage that has held continuously from the past to the reference time, satisfying the set_to_1_if conditions.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have never been in bondage' denotes a stative situation (the state of not being in bondage) without a construed boundary or transition, and the negation with 'never' (a negation of the entire perfect span) profiles an unbounded state rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have never been in bondage' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world (a fact about their past and present condition), with no modal auxiliary, conditional construction, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram includes 'been' followed by a prepositional phrase functioning as a progressive-like construction ('have never been in bondage'), which instantiates the progressive perfect form and thus encodes tense/mood specification beyond simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the prepositional phrase 'in bondage', a non-verbal predicate, making 'been' a lexical copula rather than a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness (seeing something as a completed occurrence), which falls under the 'set_to_0_if' condition for perceptual events, and lacks the dynamic, durative, homogeneous unfolding required for AktAct.

FullText: Jesus said : » If God were your Father you would love me . I came from God ! I have not come from myself . He sent me !

AktState_explanation: The gram 'have not come' denotes a bounded dynamic event (coming/arrival) marking a change of state or transition, which involves eventive unfolding and thus fails the stative criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' presents a factual statement about what actually occurred (Jesus's actual origin), not a situation contrary to fact, and does not occur in a counterfactual conditional structure.

DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' with negation ('have not come') does not entail a resulting state at the reference time; the clause negates the occurrence of the event, and no transition to a persisting state is entailed.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' describes a discrete bounded event (the origin/source of coming) rather than a situation holding continuously over time, and the verb 'come' entails a transition into a new state with focus on that transition rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have not come from myself' is negated with 'not' (not 'not yet'), but the negation of a perfective form does not itself instantiate perfectivity; rather, the negation blocks the assertion of a bounded completion, leaving the situation open and unresolved, which contradicts the perfective feature.

FullText: The Jews replied : » You are not yet fifty years old . Have you seen Abraham ? «

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have seen' is a perception verb that does not entail a resulting state holding at the reference time; it merely reports a past experiential event without a persisting change of state, similar to the negative example 'They have visited Paris.'
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (seeing Abraham) rather than a continuous situation holding over time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'Have you seen Abraham?' is negated with 'not yet' in the preceding context, indicating the transition has not occurred and the situation remains open, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have seen' appears in an interrogative form ('Have you seen Abraham?') that projects a hypothetical or impossible outcome (asking whether a human could have encountered the patriarch Abraham), presenting the situation as non-actual rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have seen' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'have seen' is active voice (agent 'you' as subject, patient 'Abraham' as object), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'had been blind' functions as a copula construction linking the subject 'he' to a predicative adjective complement ('blind'), rather than introducing an internal argument, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for copular verbs.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'be blind' is a copular construction where 'blind' is a predicative complement and the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme); no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'be blind' denotes a state or condition (change-of-state) where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing or in a state of blindness, not as an external argument initiating an activity, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for change-of-state events.
AktAcc_explanation: The target gram 'had been blind' denotes a stative condition (being blind) rather than a dynamic, durative process directed toward a defined endpoint or result state, so it does not instantiate AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'had been blind' denotes a durative state of being blind over a measurable duration rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and does not represent a boundary-crossing achievement event.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'had been blind' denotes a stative condition lacking dynamic unfolding and sustained engagement, and it entails a defined result state (the condition of blindness), which violates the core requirements for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been blind' entails a resulting state (blindness as a condition) and unfolds over measurable duration rather than occurring as a single punctual occurrence, violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
AktState_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is morphologically marked as progressive aspect (past perfect progressive), which according to set_to_0_if entails dynamic unfolding and is incompatible with stative readings, thus vetoing the stative feature AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he had been blind' presents a factual situation that actually occurred in the world of the narrative (the man was indeed blind before receiving sight), without any counterfactual marking or indication that it is contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had been blind', which is embedded in the complement clause and remains positive in its assertion.
DtDTAM_explanation: The target clause 'had been blind' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors it to a specific past time interval; the 'then' in the sentence modifies 'received his sight', not 'had been blind'.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had been blind' entails a change of state (from sighted to blind), and that resulting state of blindness held at the reference time before the man received his sight, making it a state-describing construction rather than an experiential life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The target 'had been blind' is a stative copular construction describing a prior state rather than encoding a transition to a resulting state that holds at the reference time; the edge case rule for stative copulas applies.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had been blind' denotes a non-terminating state that held continuously from a past starting point until the transition marked by 'and then received his sight', satisfying the criterion that the predicate denotes a non-terminating situation holding throughout the entire time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' has the form 'have been' + adjective (stative), profiling a state without a construed boundary or transition; the clause describes an ongoing condition rather than a bounded completed event.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been blind' is embedded in a reported belief clause ('did not believe that...') but asserts a realized past fact about the man's actual condition; it is not itself marked as modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical, and negation of the belief does not make the embedded proposition irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, which matches the first 'set_to_1_if' condition, making this a past perfect (pluperfect) rather than a simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The complement of 'been' is the adjective 'blind' (non-verbal predicate), making this a copular construction where 'been' functions as a lexical copula, not a passive auxiliary; passive voice requires 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: The Jews did not believe that he had been blind and then received his sight . So they called his parents .

ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'received' licenses an internal argument ('his sight' as direct object), triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg annotation.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Received his sight' denotes a punctual boundary transition (the moment of gaining sight), not a durative process leading to a defined endpoint, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'received his sight' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (regaining vision), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, lacking measurable internal duration, and representing a definable threshold crossing from blindness to sightedness.
AktAct_explanation: The situation 'received his sight' entails a specific, inherent endpoint (the moment sight was regained) and a defined result state (having sight), matching the set_to_0_if criterion for bounded events with completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'received his sight' entails a transition into a new resulting state (from blindness to sightedness), which violates the set_to_0_if condition that events entailing resulting change of state are not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'received his sight' denotes a bounded perceptual event marking the onset of awareness/ability (change-of-state from blindness to sight), which is a dynamic eventive change rather than a static condition, triggering the set_to_0_if rule for bounded perceptual/cognitive events.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause presents a factual situation (he received his sight) that actually occurred in the narrative world, without any counterfactual marking or indication that the event did not happen.
ClNegPol_explanation: The negation 'did not believe' applies to the matrix verb 'believe', not to the target gram 'had received his sight', which is embedded in the complement clause and is asserted as true within the Jews' belief context.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause contains the sequential temporal adverb 'then' (meaning 'at that time'), which explicitly anchors the event of receiving sight to a specific past time interval following blindness.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'received his sight' entails a change of state (from blind to sighted) where the resulting state holds at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'received' describes a change of possession/state (gaining sight from blindness), and the context 'he had been blind and then received his sight' indicates the resulting state (having sight) is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete bounded event (receiving sight) involving a transition into a new state, not a situation holding continuously across a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'received his sight' is a transition event (change from blind to sighted) construed as a completed whole, and the clause functions as a discrete step in the narrative sequence introduced by 'then', packaging the situation as a bounded occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had received his sight' is embedded in a reported belief clause ('The Jews did not believe that...') but asserts a realized fact about what actually happened in the world, not a projected or hypothetical situation; the negation applies to the Jews' belief, not to the reality of the event itself.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, forming a past perfect construction ('had received'), which instantiates TLTS as a tense specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had received his sight' is active voice (subject 'he' is the semantic agent performing the action of receiving), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: The parents said these things because they feared the Jews . The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ , he should be put out of the synagogue .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The Jews') and an overt internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), satisfying the condition for a two-participant transitive construction.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'agree' licenses an external argument (the Jews as agent/initiator of the agreement), and the clause contains an overt grammatical object ('that if any man should confess him to be Christ...'), violating both set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' licenses an internal argument (the finite clause 'that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue'), which is a complement, triggering the set_to_0_if condition that the target verb licenses or realises an internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: 'Had agreed' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state of agreement without a durative process phase leading to completion, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a punctual transition into a new state of mutual commitment or consensus, naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'had agreed at that moment'), and does not permit interruption as an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a bounded cognitive event with a specific, inherent endpoint (the moment agreement was reached), not a dynamic, durative activity that unfolds over time.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had agreed' entails a transition into a resulting state (a state of agreement with consequences), not a single punctual occurrence without resulting change.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had agreed' denotes a bounded agreement event (change-of-state from non-agreement to agreement) with a definite endpoint, which is a dynamic eventive situation rather than a stative condition.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue' presents a past agreement as factually having occurred in the narrative world, using past perfect for temporal anteriority within the narrative, not to express a counterfactual situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had agreed' is not negated; it expresses a positive agreement, and the negation in the sentence ('if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out') applies to a different eventuality, not to the agreement itself.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'The Jews had agreed that if any man should confess him to be Christ, he should be put out of the synagogue' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal past time adverbial anchoring the event to a specific past time interval; the 'if' clause is conditional, not temporal.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' entails a change in social/legal status (the Jews collectively established a binding agreement/decision) that remains in effect at the reference time, as evidenced by the subsequent conditional clause describing the ongoing consequence of that agreement.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'agree' describes a change of social/legal status (a binding agreement or decision), and the clause indicates this agreement is currently in effect as the basis for the conditional rule about confessing Christ.
DtUniv_explanation: The verb 'had agreed' denotes a discrete bounded event (a decision or agreement reached at a specific point in the past) rather than a situation holding continuously across a time span, and the focus is on the transition into the agreed state rather than duration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had agreed' is a transition event (agreement as a completed decision) construed as a bounded whole in past time, presenting a single completed occurrence rather than an ongoing or stative situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had agreed' asserts a realized past fact in the actual world (the Jews made an agreement), and while the consequent clause contains conditional/subjunctive elements ('if any man should confess...he should be put out'), the target gram itself is not marked as modal, conditional, or future, but rather as a completed past action.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past tense, which matches the first 'set_to_1_if' condition and overrides the 'set_to_0_if' condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had agreed' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'The Jews' as the semantic agent (doer) and lacks the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle'; it is a simple past perfect active construction, not a passive voice construction.

FullText: » No one takes it away from me , but I lay it down voluntarily . I have authority jurisdiction right to lay it down , and I have authority to take it again . I have received this commandment from my Father . «

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have received' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (acquiring/obtaining a commandment) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if conditions for achievement aspect.

DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have received this commandment from my Father' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have received' denotes a communication/perception event where the focus is on the occurrence of receiving a commandment as a life-history experience, without asserting that a resulting state of possession currently holds as the primary semantic point.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'receive' describes a change of possession/status (acquiring a commandment from the Father), and the context indicates this commandment is currently held and in effect as the basis for the speaker's authority to lay down and take up life.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a discrete past event (receiving a commandment) with focus on the transition into possession of that commandment, rather than a situation holding continuously from past to reference time.

TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have received' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by past participle 'received' with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the set_to_0_if condition for simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have received' is active voice with the grammatical subject 'I' as the semantic agent (the one who received), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have been' + past participle, not 'have' + past participle alone.

FullText: » My Father has given them to me . He is greater than all and no one is able to snatch them out of the Father’s hand .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses an external argument (agent 'My Father') and introduces a direct object ('them'), satisfying the set_to_0_if conditions.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has given' licenses and realizes an internal argument ('them'), triggering the set_to_0_if condition that vetoes ASUnrg annotation.

AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a completed transfer with a specific result state (them now possessed by me), entailing an inherent endpoint, and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has given' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not-possessed to possessed), which violates the set_to_0_if condition and disqualifies it from semelfactive status.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has given' denotes a bounded dynamic event involving change-of-state (transfer of possession from Father to speaker), which is incompatible with AktState; additionally, the present perfect aspect marks completion of an eventive action rather than a static condition.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has given' is not negated; the negation in the sentence ('no one is able to snatch') applies to a different eventuality and does not negate the giving event.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'My Father has given them to me' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has given' entails a change of possession (transfer of objects to the subject) that remains in effect at the reference time, triggering the set_to_0_if condition for resulting states in possession.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'give' describes a change of possession, but the clause reports a past interaction without entailing a currently persisting state of possession (the objects were given in the past, and their current location/status is described separately in the following clause).
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'has given them to me' describes a discrete bounded event (the act of giving) resulting in a new state, focusing on the transition rather than a situation holding continuously over time, matching the negative example pattern 'I have broken the vase.'
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has given them to me' presents a completed transfer event with a construed endpoint (the giving is finished), construing the transition of possession as a single bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has given' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.

FullText: Jesus replied to them : » I have shown you many good works from the Father . For which of those works do you stone me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have shown' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you many good works'), satisfying the first 'set_to_1_if' condition for a transitive two-participant event directed from agent to patient.

AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have shown' denotes a durative process of displaying or demonstrating works over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and it can naturally be modified by 'finish' (e.g., 'finished showing'), indicating completion of an extended activity phase.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have shown' is not negated; it is a positive assertion of a completed action, and no negator applies to this verb.
DtDTAM_explanation: The clause 'I have shown you many good works from the Father' contains no overt lexical, phrasal, or clausal temporal adverbial that anchors the event to a specific past time interval.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have shown' denotes a communication/demonstration event where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (displaying works) at least once in Jesus's history with the audience, rather than on a resulting state, and the clause contains the frequency modifier 'many' indicating multiple distinct instances.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'show' does not entail a resulting state that holds at the reference time; it reports a past interaction (demonstrating works) without a persisting state currently in effect, similar to the negative example 'He has given me advice before.'

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have shown' is in present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (Jesus has performed good works), with no modal, conditional, future, or hypothetical context present.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have shown' is formed with present-tense 'have' followed by a past participle with no modal, future, or progressive marking, matching the canonical present perfect form that sets the feature to 0.
VcPass_explanation: The grammatical subject 'I' corresponds to the semantic agent (the doer of showing), and the grammatical object 'you' corresponds to the semantic patient; this is active voice, not passive voice, as there is no 'been' auxiliary and no by-phrase.

FullText: Then he told them : » Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep . I go to wake him out of sleep . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has fallen asleep' is intransitive with no overt internal argument (direct or indirect object); it describes a state change without an affected patient, matching the set_to_0_if condition for verbs without overt internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'fallen asleep' denotes a change-of-state event where the sole argument 'Lazarus' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a transition to sleep, with no external argument licensed.
